EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as snow during the early morning hours across the mountains and foothills of South Carolina. The snow became moderate to heavy at times by sunrise. As much as 3 inches of snow had accumulated by 6 am. Snow, heavy at times continued during the mid and late morning hours across the mountains and foothills, with heavy snowfall totals occurring in most locations. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain over the foothills. Total snowfall accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were reported across the area by the time the snow tapered off around noon.

Isolated severe storms develop during the late afternoon and evening hours over Upstate South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees blown down along highway 66.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow began around sunrise across the eastern piedmont of South Carolina. The precipitation became heavy at times during mid-morning before mixing with sleet and freezing rain. By late morning, up to 3 inches of snow had accumulated across the area, while some locations received light accumulations of sleet. A mix of sleet and freezing rain continued across the piedmont through early afternoon. By late morning, up to an eigth of an inch of ice and as much as a half inch of sleet had accumulated on top of 2-3 inches of snow. By early afternoon, most of the precipitation had transitioned to rain.
Isolated severe storms develop during the late afternoon and evening hours over Upstate South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported along highway 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated microburst affected Spartanburg County during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large manufacturing building under construction (about 90 percent complete) near the highway 11/I-26 intersection collapsed due to strong winds. Some construction equipment was heavily damaged or destroyed by the collapsing building. Large tree limbs were also blown down nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began during the pre-dawn hours as light snow across much of the Upstate. The precipitation became heavy at times around sunrise, and began to mix with sleet and freezing rain. By mid-morning, as much as 2 inches of snow had fallen, with light accumulations of sleet reported in some areas. A mix of sleet and freezing rain continued across the Upstate through much of the morning. By mid-morning, up to a quarter inch of ice and as much as a half inch of sleet had accumulated on top of 1-3 inches of snow. By late morning, most of the precipitation had transitioned to rain.

The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beaufort County Emergency Operations Center reported most of  downtown Beaufort was flooded.  Additionally, 2 feet of standing water was reported on Highway 21 on St. Helena Island.  Sea Island Parkway and Seaside Road were both reported to be closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November provided no relief from the effects of the long term drought. In fact, another month of well-below normal rainfall made an already dire situation even worse. Many locations remained on pace to set annual records for rainfall deficit. Portions of Upstate South Carolina were as much as 20 inches below normal for the year. By the end of the month, the vast majority of the region was experiencing exceptional drought conditions. Streamflow on area rivers remained extremely low,  generally less than 10 percent of normal. Meanwhile, lakes continued to gradually fall toward record low levels, including Lake Hartwell, which was  only 3-4 feet above the record by the end of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Severe weather event was two fold, with a early morning event and an afternoon event. A frontal boundary pushed southward through the region in the early morning hours resulting in scattered thunderstorms across the region. In the afternoon, numerous residual boundaries from overnight convection in combination with the seabreeze and adequate instability resulted in another round of thunderstorms developing across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six trees were reported down in the vicinity of Highway 17.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This Tornado overturned 3 mobile homes and shifted another 7 mobile homes off there foundations.  It tore the skirting away from another mobile home, destroyed a storage shed, and snapped off or uprooted dozens of trees.  A couple of trees fell on a house.  A vehicle and an outbuilding were also damaged.  There were 9 minor injuries.  Winds with this Tornado were estimated to be 100 mph using the Enhanced Fujita Scale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually dry weather continued across the Upstate and northwest Piedmont of South Carolina through October. Although a soaking rain near the end of the month resulted in near-normal monthly precipitation for the mountains, the Piedmont saw another month of well-below normal rainfall. Most areas were on pace to break yearly rainfall deficit records. By the end of the month, exceptional drought conditions were reported across the majority of the area. Water flow on area streams continued at 3 to 6 percent of normal, while lake levels remained at near-record lows. Although most cities and towns were requesting voluntary water restrictions be observed, mandatory restrictions were ordered in quite a few communities. Also, private wells were beginning to dry up in many areas. Agriculture continued to be severely impacted by the drought. The counties comprising Upstate South Carolina were declared a federal disaster area during the month due to agricultural losses. At this time, county by county dollar estimates of drought damage have not been made available.

Strong Upper Level shortwave trough in combination with sea breeze resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level short wave trough and an associated pre-frontal surface trough moving into a very unstable airmass resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees were reported down at Summers Bind near the intersection of Ashley Phosphate and Dorchester Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level short wave trough and an associated pre-frontal surface trough moving into a very unstable airmass resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half to three inch diameter tree branches were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary in combination with sea breeze and numerous low level boundaries in a highly unstable environment resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water was reported over the road at the intersection of Ashley Phosphate Road and Dunlap Street. Secondary roadways were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary in combination with sea breeze and numerous low level boundaries in a highly unstable environment resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half to three feet of water were reported over the road at the intersection of Market Street and Church Street in downtown Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass in combination with a strong upper level low digging southward from the appalachians, allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down at the Allendale County Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on State Road 47.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level low pressure and unstable environment led to numerous severe thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four large trees were reported down along the Broad River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong cold pool aloft, along with a weak surface frontal boundary and afternoon sea breeze, resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail and 40 to 50 mph wind gusts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful coastal storm off in the Atlantic produced high winds and dangerous surf conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two kayakers went into the surf off of Kiawah Island.  They were swept offshore where the kayak overturned.  One person was rescued 18 hours later.  The other perished and the body was recovered on May 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary in combination with sea breeze allowed a few isolated thunderstorms to develop across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down on Sandy Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of northeastern South Carolina saw virtually no rainfall for the month, deepening a lingering drought across the region. The US Drought Monitor scale cited in the county events is here: http://drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued lack of rainfall exacerbated already dry conditions with the United States Drought Monitor classifying most of the region as Extreme (D3), with areas near the coast as Severe (D2). For the year, most areas had only received between 40-70 percent of normal rainfall, with daily stream flows dropping to only the 5th percentile. Most of the water systems in the region were under restrictions, with burning bans in place.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued lack of rainfall exacerbated already dry conditions with the United States Drought Monitor classifying most of the region as Extreme (D3), with areas near the coast as Severe (D2). For the year, most areas had only received between 40-70 percent of normal rainfall, with daily stream flows dropping to only the 5th percentile. Most of the water systems in the region were under restrictions, with burning bans in place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with strong dynamics and windshear pushed into the area late Saturday and early Sunday.  Hail and microbursts accompanied the first wave of storms while the second wave of storms produced a half dozen tornadoes.  The largest was an EF3 that killed on person and injured 3 others.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF3 tornado traveled from southwest to northeast of Sumter destroying a seven mobile homes, severely damaging nine framed homes, and doing light to moderate damage to an additional 62 mobile and framed homes. There was 1 fatality and 3 injuries.  The tornado touched down as an EF1 and ended as an EF3 where the fatality occurred at a mobile home on hwy 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions persisted across the Upstate and northwest Piedmont of South Carolina through September, as the region experienced another month of well-below normal precipitation. By the end of the month, most locations were running a yearly rainfall deficit of 11-17 inches. Stream flows and groundwater levels were near record low levels, with many streams running at 5 percent or less of normal flow. Water levels on area reservoirs were some of the lowest in recorded history. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Farmers continued to struggle to feed livestock due to a lack of hay and poor pasture conditions, forcing many cattle to be sold or slaughtered. Agricultural and other losses attributed to the drought are estimated to be in the hundreds of millions of dollars. County-based losses for the growing season will be included in next month's Storm Data.

The effects of an extended period of dry weather were exacerbated by an abnormally dry May, with many locations reporting one of the driest Mays in recorded history. By the end of May, many climatological stations were reporting yearly rainfall deficits as high as 10 inches. The result was severe to extreme drought conditions across much of western Upstate South Carolina by the end of the month. The very dry conditions added to agriculture hardships caused by a hard freeze and widespread damaging winds in April.

The very dry conditions added to agriculture hardships caused by a hard freeze and widespread damaging winds in April.

An early spring hard freeze saw temperatures fall to the upper teens and lower 20s across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia on the morning of the 8th. This resulted in massive agricultural losses across the region. It was estimated that 90 percent of the apple and peach crop across the area was destroyed. As much as 50 percent of the berry crop was lost, while more than 50 percent of the grape crop across western North Carolina was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The latter half of December saw a transition to a wetter pattern across the southeast. Most observing stations in the Upstate and northwest Piedmont of South Carolina reported above normal monthly rainfall for the first time since January 2007. However, this was not enough to put much of a dent in the long-term drought as extreme to exceptional drought conditions persisted into the New Year. Despite the wet end to the year, portions of Upstate South Carolina experienced the driest year on record. Although the increase in rainfall did allow for some recharge of area streams, most were still running at less than 25 percent of normal flow at the end of the month. Lake Hartwell recorded the 3rd lowest December average pool level on record.

Rainfall through mid-December continued below normal, exacerbating the drought across northeastern South Carolina. Some timely rainfall toward the end of the month improved conditions slightly. The US Drought Monitor scale cited in the county events is here: http://drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued lack of rainfall exacerbated drought conditions with the United States Drought Monitor classifying most of the region as Severe (D2), and areas west of Interstate 95 Extreme (D3). For the year, most areas received about 70 percent of normal rainfall, with daily stream flows dropping at or below the 5th percentile. Most of the water systems in the region were under restrictions, with burning bans in place. Some rainfall at the end of the month improved conditions slightly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the coast of South Carolina and Georgia as a potent upper level disturbance moved across the region. Moderate to heavy rains fell across the area, bringing much needed rainfall to some drought stricken areas. The pressure gradient between the area of low pressure and a wedge of high pressure across the piedmont of South Carolina and Georgia, associated with strong cold air damming, produced strong winds along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked down a large oak tree across Camp Road on James Island. Sustained winds of 46 mph with gusts as high as 55 mph were measured by the C-Man station on Folly Beach. Additionally, power lines were blown down by high winds at the intersection of Folly Road and Fort Johnson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The system of severe thunderstorms that produced deadly tornadoes across the gulf states moved up through Georgia and into South Carolina producing wind damage from downbursts and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several trees down on Border Drive and Haskel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The system of severe thunderstorms that produced deadly tornadoes across the gulf states moved up through Georgia and into South Carolina producing wind damage from downbursts and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees and some powerlines down countywide along with minor damage to a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The system of severe thunderstorms that produced deadly tornadoes across the gulf states moved up through Georgia and into South Carolina producing wind damage from downbursts and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 took down trees and did minor damage to a couple of homes along Talatha road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front pushed through the low country bringing very strong winds throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell on a house in a residential area of Knightsville.  Some roof damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper shortwave was moving east-northeast from the Gulf of Mexico during the late afternoon and into the evening, with a moist and unstable air mass at the surface. At the same time, a 130 knot upper jet lifted northward from the Gulf of Mexico. This combination of moisture, instability, and strong wind fields aloft allowed for the development of organized severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down on Highway 3 and Highway 278 about 1 mile east of Allendale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted or snapped off a few trees and broke off large tree limbs in several locations from 1 mile south of Allendale to 1.5 miles northeast of Sycamore. The damage 1.5 miles northeast of Sycamore occurred around 1912 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with strong dynamics and windshear pushed into the area late Saturday and early Sunday.  Hail and microbursts accompanied the first wave of storms while the second wave of storms produced a half dozen tornadoes.  The largest was an EF3 that killed on person and injured 3 others.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees down on hwys 283, 230, and Plum Branch road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave came down out of the northwest and produced low top convection.  Cold temps aloft caused hail to develop in several of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported nickel size hail northwest of Bowman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 touched down SE of Rowesville and traveled NE to just N of Bowman.  Trees and a couple of powerlines were taken down along the path on SR 38-80, Classic Rd, Turkeyend St, Candlewood Ct, Dibble st, and SR 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved rapidly across the upstate. The line produced damaging winds and a few non-supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down very briefly in the parking lot of the Liberty Elementary School. Nine vehicles were severely damaged. At least 4 vehicles were lifted off the ground or flipped over by the wind. Some of the vehicles were occupied by parents awaiting school dismissal. Fifteen people received mostly minor injuries, with 5 being transported to the hospital. A shed was also blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pine trees were blown down and minor roof damage was reported to several homes along George Bridge Rd and Thompson Quarters Rd in extreme northwest Chester County. One tree fell on and destroyed a barn.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Hebron Dunbar Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp upper trough was located over eastern Tennessee. The associated surface low tracked northeast across western and central South Carolina with a 60 knot low level jet.  Vertical shear was favorable for organized storms including supercells and bow echoes.  A tornado watch was in effect with the primary threat damaging winds and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on a house. At least 2500 people were without power due to fallen trees on power lines. Damage estimated.

Moderate to heavy rain fell for much of the afternoon and evening on the 1st, producing around 3 inches of rain by late evening across the northern part of the Upstate. This resulted in gradually rising water levels on several small streams and creeks, with several streams leaving their banks by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Greenville County included the Reedy River near Greenville, the Enoree River near Taylors, and the Middle Tyger River north of Greer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain fell for much of the afternoon and evening on the 1st, producing around 3 inches of rain by late evening across the northern part of the Upstate. This resulted in gradually rising water levels on several small streams and creeks, with several streams leaving their banks by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Spartanburg County included the Pacolet River near the North Carolina border, which flooded Landrum Rd. The Middle Tyger River also flooded Mt Lebanon Church Rd north of Greer. There was also some road flooding due to poor drainage, including Amber Dr and Old Mill Rd in Inman and Eli Cox Rd in Enoree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain fell for much of the afternoon and evening on the 1st, producing around 3 inches of rain by late evening across the northern part of the Upstate. This resulted in gradually rising water levels on several small streams and creeks, with several streams leaving their banks by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Pickens County included portions of the Saluda River and Gregory Creek, which flooded the bridge over Concord Church Rd near its intersection with Massingill Memorial. Mauldin Lake Rd was also under water, apparently due to flooding along Wolf Creek. Water was also over highway 183 in several places, mainly due to poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms produced penny to golf ball size hail across much of the CWA and covered the ground in portions of Lancaster County.  Downburst winds also took down some trees and powerlines.  An EF0 tornado touched down in the White Oak area of Fairfield County with one large tree crushing two vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 3/4 inch hail in Elgin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary near the North Carolina state line and pushed south through the Pee Dee and Midlands producing large hail and some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golfball size hail on Pine Plain Road in both Lexington and Calhoun counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough, very unstable air, and an upper air disturbance moving east all combined to produce tornadoes and microbursts across a large portion of northeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey concluded an EF1 Tornado with winds between 100 and 110 mph caused damage along a path 100 yards long and 10 yards wide on D Street, just off of US Highway 501. Damage was very localized. A few softwood trees were snapped at the trunk, and a large oak tree was heavily damaged. A house lost siding and had one broken window, and its well house was tossed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms developed over much of the CWA producing large hail and many microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported trees down, damage to a house and nursery.  A carport and trampoline were destroyed.  All this occurred in the vicinity of Plowder Mill Rd, Relief Rd, Ike Brunson Rd, and Bart Davis Ln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms developed over portions of the CWA and produced hail up to quarter size and several microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported large limbs down and roof damage to Midway Convenience Store in the Flat Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper shortwave was moving east-northeast from the Gulf of Mexico during the late afternoon and into the evening, with a moist and unstable air mass at the surface. At the same time, a 130 knot upper jet lifted northward from the Gulf of Mexico. This combination of moisture, instability, and strong wind fields aloft allowed for the development of organized severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed about 1 mile southeast of Allendale and traveled north-northeast about 60 yards before lifting. The tornado was about 30 yards wide and had maximum wind speeds estimated to be 90 mph. This puts the tornado in the low end of the F1 category. This tornado uprooted trees and damaged the skirting of a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Phasing of jet streams allowed for a rapidly developing low pressure system. Large scale dynamics and instability allowed for several low topped supercells to develop primarily along and ahead of a cold front over portions of western counties of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This Tornado snapped off or uprooted hundreds of trees...destroyed a large framed barn with debris spread over a half mile to the northeast.  A large object hit a house breaking out three windows and knocking some of the bricks loose.  Siding was also torn from the house.  A large 8000 pound horse trailer was lifted up and carried 500 feet.  An 800 pound grain wagon was lifted and carried 400 feet before landing on the ground.  A bass boat was flipped over and a 4 wheeler was damaged along with other farm equipment.  A tree also fell on a mobile home.  Winds with this Tornado were estimated to be 110 mph using the new Enhanced Fujita Scale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Phasing of jet streams allowed for a rapidly developing low pressure system. Large scale dynamics and instability allowed for several low topped supercells to develop primarily along and ahead of a cold front over portions of western counties of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This Tornado carried two empty grain silos about 100 feet, snapped off or uprooted dozens of trees, destroyed a large portion of an outbuilding, and severely injured a dog.  Maximum sustained winds with this Tornado were estimated to be 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave moved across the area and produced multicellular convection with large hail and microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring hard freeze saw temperatures fall to the upper teens and lower 20s across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia on the morning of the 8th. This resulted in massive agricultural losses across the region. It was estimated that 90 percent of the apple and peach crop across the area was destroyed. As much as 50 percent of the berry crop was lost, while more than 50 percent of the grape crop across western North Carolina was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an intense, but relatively brief high wind event affected the mountains and foothills on the evening of the 15th, another widespread damaging high wind event developed during the day of the 16th. However, this particular event included much of the piedmont. Thousands of trees fell across the area, resulting in widespread power outages.  Numerous trees fell on roads, homes, and vehicles. A 90-year-old man in Walhalla, SC was killed when a tree fell on and crushed the outbuilding he was in.
Isolated severe thunderstorms produced damage, with one lightning injury.

An early spring hard freeze saw temperatures fall to the upper teens and lower 20s across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia on the morning of the 8th. This resulted in massive agricultural losses across the region. It was estimated that 90 percent of the apple and peach crop across the area was destroyed. As much as 50 percent of the berry crop was lost, while more than 50 percent of the grape crop across western North Carolina was damaged.

A late season cold snap produced widespread frost and freezing temperatures across much of South Carolina. Overall damage to the state was at least a 100 million dollar loss due to damaged fruit and vegetable plants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dipped down into the 20s most areas which produced widespread damage to crops and fruit trees.  Total monetary losses unknown but significant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an intense, but relatively brief high wind event affected the mountains and foothills on the evening of the 15th, another widespread damaging high wind event developed during the day of the 16th. However, this particular event included much of the piedmont. Thousands of trees fell across the area, resulting in widespread power outages.  Numerous trees fell on roads, homes, and vehicles. A 90-year-old man in Walhalla, SC was killed when a tree fell on and crushed the outbuilding he was in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening High pressure over New England and low pressure off the Southeast U.S. coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across southern South Carolina and Southeast Georgia.  Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce significant Beach Erosion across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening High pressure over New England and low pressure off the Southeast U.S. coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across southern South Carolina and Southeast Georgia.  Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce significant Beach Erosion across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high pressure building into the Southeast U.S. and low pressure off the South Carolina and Georgia coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across the area. Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce moderate to severe Beach Erosion and some Coastal Flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to Severe Beach Erosion and Coastal Flooding took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters. Ocean overwash was observed on Porpoise Street. Parts of Fripp Island Golf Course was flooded at high tide. Parts of Cabin Road were washed out at high tide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high pressure building into the Southeast U.S. and low pressure off the South Carolina and Georgia coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across the area. Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce moderate to severe Beach Erosion and some Coastal Flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to Severe Beach Erosion and Coastal Flooding took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters. Water from Bay Back Creek was reported to be approaching houses between the 1400 and 1500 block of Palmetto Boulevard. Docks and the front yards of several houses were submerged by Bay Back Creek. Moderate to Severe Beach Erosion was reported at Edisto Beach.  Water was reported in the parking lot of Dockside Restaurant. The front yards of several houses on Scott Creek Drive and Jungle Shores Drive were reported to be flooded, with the water reaching the houses. Steamboat Landing Road was reported flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across much of the upstate and northwest Piedmont of South Carolina. Stream flows and groundwater levels approached record low levels. Water levels on some reservoirs decreased by as much as 1 foot every 10 days. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Dollar values for the drought damage should be included in either the September or October Storm Data for this region.

Strengthening High pressure over New England and low pressure off the Southeast U.S. coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across southern South Carolina and Southeast Georgia.  Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce significant Beach Erosion across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters. Significant Beach Erosion was reported at both the Isle of Palms and Wild Dunes. Boardwalks were damaged or destroyed. Several Homes and Condos at Wild Dunes were damaged by high surf during times of high tide.  The 18th hole on a golf course was completely washed away.  Significant Beach Erosion was also reported at Folly Beach, where much of the beach renurishment project was washed back into the sea. Water was up to the dune line at times of high tide.

A frontal boundary dropping south, combined with an upper level disturbance ignited multiple severe thunderstorms over several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey concluded that the damage examined was the result of straight line winds. A north-south swath of damage was found crossing SC Highway 9 near Duford. Damage was mainly in the form of large snapped trees and peeled off metal roofing from sheds and barns. Damage was also scattered from just north of S.R. 9 from Mount Olive Church Road to Bay View Drive. Considerable damage occurred at Green Sea Floyds High School including minor roof damage, a downed light pole, damage to a tennis court fence, broken windows, several trees down and a well constructed football scoreboard was blown over. An 18 wheel tractor trailer was overturned on SC Highway 9. Winds were estimated between 60 and 90 mph. A small area of more concentrated damage occurred along Mount Olive Church Road between Billy MC Road and Fairview Highway. At this location, a mobile home was completely destroyed and seven occupants in this home suffered minor injuries and were taken to the local hospital. Large trees 3-4 ft diameter were snapped and uprooted. Wind speeds with this damage were estimated between 95 and 109 mph. Two firefighters were injured when their firetruck ran over a downed tree in the road. All of the damage observed was aligned from north-northwest to south-southeast. About 10,000 people lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary dropping south, combined with an upper level disturbance ignited multiple severe thunderstorms over several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Cedar Street. Isolated downed trees were observed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary dropping south, combined with an upper level disturbance ignited multiple severe thunderstorms over several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew trees and power lines down. One large oak tree fell through a bedroom window of a home on Rutledge Lane in the Burgess community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Swells from Hurricane Dean produced strong rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Horry County Beach Services reported combined 56 rip current related rescues at county beaches. There were no drownings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary dropping south, combined with an upper level disturbance ignited multiple severe thunderstorms over several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell, severely damaging a vehicle. Tree caused minor damage to the exterior of a home on Henry Street, cracking a window and separating the electrical meter from the wall. Nickel size hail also fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm formed along an outflow and became briefly severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near Alligator Road and Twin Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A single thunderstorm had a downburst that caused moderate damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst produced damage along a path 2 miles long and up to a half mile wide. Damage was more destuctive along a 100 foot path. Several trees up to 2 feet in diameter were snapped 12 to 20 feet above the ground. A line of four power poles were leaning one way and trees snapped the other way. Ten stuctures sustained minor damage to include roof shingles and mobile home underpinnings. One tree fell onto a house and brick pillars on the porch were knocked down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff and public reported 3/4 inch hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported several trees down in Batesburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported several trees down in Graniteville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aiken County airport measured a gust to 50kts on AWOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down across the intersection of Rt 9 and 41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A myriad of boundaries in combination with sea breeze in a highly unstable environment and fairly strong flow aloft, resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a power line in the Ridgeland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 4/12, in the early morning hours, a line of severe thunderstorms produced some hail up to nickel size and some minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visitor to office reported nickel size hail at her home southwest of Pomeria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unstable air mass produced a severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines down west of SR 41 and north of SSR 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe storms affected Upstate South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees blown down on Woodruff Rd in Greer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms produced isolated wind damage over Upstate and the Western Piedmont of South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines blown down in the Clover area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected the Upstate of South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large limbs blown down, and some gutters torn off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze and became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell at the intersection of SR 151 and US Hwy 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved rapidly across the upstate. The line produced damaging winds and a few non-supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short damage track began on Bethany Church Rd northwest of Moore and extended northeast before ending on highway 290. Two sheds were destroyed and the roof partially blown off a larger shed. Part of a deck was torn off a house. Also, several 8 inch diameter pine trees were snapped and numerous limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced wind gusts to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated a wind gust to 60 mph in Duford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level short wave trough and an associated pre-frontal surface trough moving into a very unstable airmass resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few Trees were reported down at the 26 mile marker of Northbound Interstate 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating in combination with sea breeze allowed a few thunderstorms to develop across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Pine tree brought down powerlines at the intersection of Ritter Road and Blue House Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced microburst across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported an intense microburst destroyed a 40'X 60' building, took down many trees (one split a home), took siding off a few homes, and twisted metal fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave moved across the area and produced multicellular convection with large hail and microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported nickel to quarter size hail and powerlines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered pulse storms produced hail and damaging winds across portions of the midlands and CSRA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gilbert FD reported two trees down on Harley Taylor road and US 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms developed ahead and along a cold front that dropped south into the state producing small hail and some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Post Office reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Severe weather event was two fold, with a early morning event and an afternoon event. A frontal boundary pushed southward through the region in the early morning hours resulting in scattered thunderstorms across the region. In the afternoon, numerous residual boundaries from overnight convection in combination with the seabreeze and adequate instability resulted in another round of thunderstorms developing across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle size hail was reported to have fallen for 10 minutes at the Quick Stop on Byrnes Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms in southwest Barnwell county produced some locally heave rains with urban and small stream flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Savannah River Site reported water flowing over road B and road 7 after several inches of rain fell over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected the Upstate of South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A traffic light was blown down on Pleasantburg Dr. Part of a large tree was blown onto an automobile at Pelham Rd and the Parkway. Also, |quite a few trees and power lines were blown down around the county.
